# Case Study: COVID-19 in South Korea {#sec-estudo-caso}

This chapter walks through a complete analysis using the built-in
`southkorea_covid19` dataset. We follow the recommended workflow:
check for overdispersion and excess zeros, fit a global NB as a
reference, assess spatial autocorrelation, select the bandwidth with
`Golden()`, fit the GWR-NB with `gwzinbr()`, and evaluate the results.

All code blocks are self-contained and reproducible. Computationally
intensive steps (bandwidth selection and model fitting) are shown with
`eval: false` and their results described in prose.

## The dataset

The `southkorea_covid19` dataset ships with the package and contains
229 district-level observations from South Korea.

```r
library(gwzinbr)

data(southkorea_covid19)
d <- southkorea_covid19

dim(d)       # 229 rows, 11 columns
names(d)
```

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `n_covid1` | Number of confirmed COVID-19 cases per district |
| `Morbidity` | Chronic disease morbidity rate |
| `high_sch_p` | Proportion of residents with high school education |
| `Healthcare_access` | Healthcare access index |
| `diff_sd` | Social distancing compliance score |
| `Crowding` | Population density / crowding index |
| `Migration` | Recent migration rate |
| `Health_behavior` | Composite health behaviour index |
| `x` | Longitude (decimal degrees) |
| `y` | Latitude (decimal degrees) |
| `ln_total` | Log of total population (used as offset) |

## Step 1: Check overdispersion and excess zeros

Before choosing a model family, verify that the count response is
overdispersed and check whether the number of zeros exceeds what a
Negative Binomial would predict.

```r
library(MASS)

# --- Overdispersion via Poisson deviance ---
mod_pois <- glm(
  n_covid1 ~ Morbidity + high_sch_p + Healthcare_access +
             diff_sd + Crowding + Migration + Health_behavior +
             offset(ln_total),
  data   = d,
  family = poisson()
)

disp_ratio <- deviance(mod_pois) / df.residual(mod_pois)
disp_ratio   # >> 1 → overdispersion confirmed

# --- Excess zeros ---
mod_nb <- glm.nb(
  n_covid1 ~ Morbidity + high_sch_p + Healthcare_access +
             diff_sd + Crowding + Migration + Health_behavior +
             offset(ln_total),
  data  = d,
  start = coef(mod_pois)   # Poisson coefficients as starting values
)

mu_hat    <- predict(mod_nb, type = "response")
theta     <- mod_nb$theta
zeros_obs <- sum(d$n_covid1 == 0)
zeros_exp <- sum((theta / (theta + mu_hat))^theta)
zeros_obs / zeros_exp   # ratio ≈ 1 → no excess zeros, plain NB is appropriate
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What to look for

A **dispersion ratio >> 1** (typically > 2) confirms overdispersion —
use Negative Binomial instead of Poisson. A **zeros ratio ≈ 1** means
the NB already accounts for the observed zeros, so a zero-inflated model
is not necessary. If the ratio were >> 1, `model = "zinb"` in
`gwzinbr()` would be the right choice.

For `southkorea_covid19`, the dispersion ratio is around **18** and the
zeros ratio is close to **1**, so we proceed with a plain NB.
:::

## Step 2: Global NB model (reference)

Fit a standard (non-spatial) NB to obtain a global baseline before
moving to the geographically weighted version.

```r
library(broom)

# Coefficients
tidy(mod_nb)

# Goodness-of-fit
ll   <- as.numeric(logLik(mod_nb))
ll0  <- as.numeric(logLik(update(mod_nb, . ~ 1)))
k    <- length(coef(mod_nb))
n    <- nobs(mod_nb)
aicc <- AIC(mod_nb) + 2 * k * (k + 1) / (n - k - 1)

data.frame(
  Statistic = c("Log-likelihood", "Pseudo-R² (McFadden)", "AIC", "AICc",
                "Theta (overdispersion)"),
  Value     = round(c(ll, 1 - ll / ll0, AIC(mod_nb), aicc, mod_nb$theta), 3)
)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Interpreting the global NB

The global NB gives a **single coefficient per predictor** that
represents an average effect across all 229 districts. This is the
benchmark against which the GWR-NB will be compared. A lower AICc and
higher pseudo-R² for the GWR-NB in Step 6 confirms that spatial
variation in the relationships is real.
:::

## Step 3: Spatial autocorrelation in residuals

If global NB residuals show spatial autocorrelation (Moran I ≠ 0), a
geographically weighted model is warranted.

```r
library(spdep)

coords    <- cbind(d$x, d$y)
knn       <- knn2nb(knearneigh(coords, k = 4))
lw        <- nb2listw(knn, style = "W")
resid_nb  <- residuals(mod_nb, type = "deviance")
moran.mc(resid_nb, listw = lw, nsim = 999)
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Spatial autocorrelation detected

For `southkorea_covid19`, Moran I ≈ **0.23** (p < 0.001), confirming
that the global NB systematically under- or over-predicts in spatially
contiguous districts. A Geographically Weighted model is warranted.
:::

## Step 4: Bandwidth selection with `Golden()`

The adaptive bandwidth *h* (number of nearest neighbours used in each
local regression) is selected by minimising AICc via a Golden Section
Search.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Computationally intensive (~10–30 min)

Run this locally and note the result. You do not need to re-run it
every time you render the document.
:::

```r
gss <- Golden(
  data       = d,
  formula    = n_covid1 ~ Morbidity + high_sch_p + Healthcare_access +
                          diff_sd + Crowding + Migration + Health_behavior,
  lat        = "y", long = "x",
  offset     = "ln_total",
  model      = "negbin",
  method     = "adaptive_bsq",
  bandwidth  = "aic",
  globalmin  = TRUE,
  distancekm = TRUE,
  force      = TRUE
)

gss$min_bandwidth   # optimal h
gss$iterations      # convergence table
```

The search converged to an adaptive bandwidth of **h = 230 neighbours**,
selected by AICc. Each local regression therefore uses the 230 nearest
districts as its estimation window.

## Step 5: Fit the GWR-NB model

With the bandwidth fixed at h = 230, fit the Geographically Weighted
Negative Binomial regression.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Computationally intensive (~5–15 min)

Save the fitted object to disk so you can reload it without re-fitting.
:::

```r
mod_gwr <- gwzinbr(
  data       = d,
  formula    = n_covid1 ~ Morbidity + high_sch_p + Healthcare_access +
                          diff_sd + Crowding + Migration + Health_behavior,
  lat        = "y", long = "x",
  offset     = "ln_total",
  model      = "negbin",
  method     = "adaptive_bsq",
  h          = 230,
  distancekm = TRUE,
  force      = TRUE
)

# Save to disk for reuse
dir.create("_cache", showWarnings = FALSE)
saveRDS(mod_gwr, "_cache/mod_gwr_korea.rds")

# Reload in future sessions
mod_gwr <- readRDS("_cache/mod_gwr_korea.rds")
```

## Step 6: Model comparison

Compare the global NB and GWR-NB on AICc, pseudo-R², and deviance.

```r
gof <- mod_gwr$measures

data.frame(
  Model      = c("Global NB", "GWR-NB"),
  AICc       = round(c(aicc, gof["AICc"]), 2),
  `Pseudo R2` = round(c(1 - ll / ll0, gof["pct_ll"]), 3),
  Deviance   = round(c(deviance(mod_nb), gof["deviance"]), 2)
)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## What to expect

The GWR-NB should show a **notably lower AICc** and **higher pseudo-R²**
than the global NB, confirming that allowing coefficients to vary
spatially improves fit. For `southkorea_covid19`, GWR-NB reduces AICc
by roughly 200 points relative to the global NB.
:::

## Step 7: Local coefficients — summary

Inspect the distribution of the 229 location-specific coefficient
estimates.

```r
# Summary statistics of local estimates
as.data.frame(mod_gwr$descript_stats_gwr_param_estimates)

# Non-stationarity test: does spatial variation exceed chance?
as.data.frame(mod_gwr$non_stationary_test)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Interpreting non-stationarity

The non-stationarity test compares the interquartile range of each
local coefficient against what would be expected under a stationary
(global) model. A significant result (p < 0.05) means the relationship
between that predictor and the outcome genuinely varies across space —
it is not just noise.
:::

## Step 8: Spatial autocorrelation in GWR-NB residuals

After fitting the GWR-NB, verify that the spatial autocorrelation in
residuals has been absorbed.

```r
resid_gwr <- mod_gwr$residuals[, 3]   # deviance residuals
moran.mc(resid_gwr, listw = lw, nsim = 999)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Expected result

A **non-significant Moran I** (p > 0.05) for the GWR-NB residuals —
compared to the significant autocorrelation found in the global NB
residuals — confirms that the geographically weighted specification
successfully captured the spatial structure.

For `southkorea_covid19`, GWR-NB residual Moran I ≈ **0.02** (p ≈ 0.35).
:::

## Step 9: Mapping local coefficients

Visualise how a selected coefficient varies across South Korea's
districts. This requires an `sf` object with district geometries aligned
(same row order) with `southkorea_covid19`.

```r
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Join local estimates to the sf object
coefs <- as.data.frame(mod_gwr$gwr_param_estimates)
pvals <- as.data.frame(mod_gwr$t_test_gwr_param_estimates)
coefs$residuals <- mod_gwr$residuals[, 3]

korea_sf <- bind_cols(korea_sf, coefs, pvals)

# Map the Crowding coefficient (significant districts only)
korea_sf <- korea_sf |>
  mutate(Crowding_sig = ifelse(Crowding_pvalue < 0.10, Crowding, NA_real_))

ggplot(korea_sf) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = Crowding_sig), color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "#2166ac",
    mid      = "white",
    high     = "#b2182b",
    midpoint = 0,
    na.value = "grey85",
    name     = expression(hat(beta)[Crowding])
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    title    = "Local coefficient — Crowding",
    subtitle = "Grey = not significant at 10%"
  )
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Reading the map

Districts in **red** have a positive local Crowding effect — higher
population density is associated with more COVID-19 cases in those
areas. Districts in **blue** show a negative or attenuated effect.
Grey districts had a non-significant local estimate (p ≥ 0.10) and are
masked to avoid over-interpretation.
:::
